policy_statement: Support agricultural and soil health instruction in schools and connect students with farms and ecological benefits of healthy soils.
mechanism_description: Supporting soil health instruction in schools and connecting students with farms builds knowledge that can encourage soil-health practices, improving ecosystem function and increasing soil carbon over time.
primary_causal_pathway: Education & outreach → adoption of soil-health practices → ↑ soil organic carbon + improved ecosystem function → ↑ carbon sequestration → ↓ net atmospheric GHG concentration
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: curricula, training, and experiential learning that influence land managers’/future farmers’ decisions toward soil-health practices (e.g., cover cropping, reduced tillage, compost additions, diversified rotations, managed grazing).  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: increased soil organic matter formation and stabilization, improved soil structure and microbial activity, reduced erosion, and enhanced nutrient/water cycling.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: net removal of CO₂ from the atmosphere via increased soil carbon stocks (with possible additional benefits like reduced N₂O risk when nutrient management improves, depending on practices adopted).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased soil carbon sequestration through wider adoption of soil-health practices encouraged by education and farm connections.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: education/outreach changes management behavior, which enhances soil/ecosystem condition and increases carbon stored in soils (a biophysical sink).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while there can be resource-efficiency or direct mitigation co-effects (e.g., lower input use), the defining and largest climate effect of this mechanism is ecosystem restoration and carbon sequestration, fitting Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: if education primarily drives input efficiency (fertilizer/energy reductions) without measurable soil carbon gains, it could lean Resource Efficiency/Mitigation; however the canonical mechanism explicitly targets “soil carbon and ecosystem restoration,” so NbS remains dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: any policy framed as soil-health education/outreach aiming at soil carbon restoration relies on ecological carbon stock enhancement as the core climate lever, regardless of location.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., specific practices like cover crops vs. compost vs. grazing) is deferred to policy-level evidence and implementation details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Education alone is an enabling action; realized climate impact depends on actual practice adoption and permanence of soil carbon. If programs emphasize productivity or nutrient efficiency without soil carbon objectives/measurement, categorization could shift, but that is outside this canonical mechanism definition.
key_indicators: “Educate students”; “support agricultural and soil health instruction in schools”; “connect students with farms and farmers”; “knowledge of the ecological benefit of healthy soils”
co_benefits: Improved agricultural workforce development and agricultural literacy; potential water-quality and erosion-reduction awareness; broader environmental stewardship education
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be classified as an information/education instrument (information-disclosure) if that category explicitly covered public education/outreach; here coded as planning-strategy because it describes a programmatic education approach without a specific disclosure requirement or binding curriculum mandate.
